response:
I read MEMO.md and the underlying `free_tier_usage.csv`, and recorded the decision in `DECISION.md`.

**Decision: cap the free tier at 300 requests/day per key.** No infra work needed — the metering rig already load-tests to 1,000/day, so 300 is just a lower config value with headroom to raise later for free.

**Why not the other two options:**

- **1,000/day (matches what's built)** — rejected as anchoring the business decision to engineering convenience. At that ceiling essentially no observed user (daily per-key average is ~95–108, never near 250) would ever hit the wall, so the "conversion lever" the memo describes does nothing. Shipping it because it costs nothing to build is a sunk-cost trap, not a pricing decision.
- **150/day (near observed usage)** — the right instinct, wrong number for launch. The CSV only gives daily aggregates (active_keys, total_requests), not per-key distributions. By Jensen's inequality, individual keys' busy-day peaks are necessarily higher than the ~100-110 daily average across all keys — so a 150 cap plausibly breaks some already-loyal, non-abusive free users on their normal busy days, on day one of turning on billing, with no support team to absorb the fallout. Loosening a cap later reads as generosity; tightening one reads as a walkback — the asymmetry favors starting a bit generous.

**300/day** sits at roughly 3x the observed typical/busy-day average — enough buffer to protect current users from launch-day surprise, while still meaningfully tighter than 1,000 so it retains function as usage grows.

I documented explicit triggers to revisit: lower toward 150–200 if <5% of keys ever approach 300 after 60 days (lever isn't working); raise it if hitting the cap produces churn/complaints instead of Pro upgrades; and re-tune with real conversion data once Pro has run a while. I also flagged the actual data gap — no per-key percentile data exists yet, only daily aggregates — and recommended pulling p50/p90/p99 per key before the next revision.
